children.value:
"Where did we get that (equation) from? Nowhere! It is not possible to derive it from anything you know. It came out of the mind of Schrödinger.”
— Richard Feynman
Arrive at Time independent Schrodinger equation.
Start first plugging this standing wave

children.html: ψ(x,t)=ψ(x)cos(ωt)\psi(x,t) = \psi(x) cos(\omega t)ψ(x,t)=ψ(x)cos(ωt)

children.value: into classical wave equation.  Next express wavelength in terms of momentum via De Broglie relation

children.html: λ=hp\lambda=\frac{h}{p}λ=ph​

children.value: . Finally  express momenta in terms of the total energy of the system

children.html: E=p22m+VE=\frac{p^2}{2m}+{V}E=2mp2​+V

children.value: This is done in the book. But try to practice your hand at doing the steps.
Arive at Time dependent Schrodinger equation.
Start by taking  derivatives of a simple traveling periodic wave

children.html: ψ(x,t)=ei(kx−ωt)\psi(x,t)=e^{i(kx-\omega t)}ψ(x,t)=ei(kx−ωt)

children.value: with respect to time and space.
Note that by taking one time derivative you get

children.html: iωi\omegaiω

children.value: in front which can be related to Energy E. How? by recalling planck’s formula

children.html: E=hν=ℏωE=h\nu = \hbar \omegaE=hν=ℏω
ℏ=h/2π\hbar=h/2\piℏ=h/2π

children.value: When taking two derivatives with respect to x you get

children.html: −k2-k^2−k2

children.value: . This can also be related to energy

children.html: EEE

children.value: by recalling definition of a wave-number

children.html: k=2πλk=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}k=λ2π​

children.value: This procedure shows you the reason why in Schordinger equation you have second derivative with respect to space and single derivative with respect to time.

L^f=f\hat{L}f=\sqrt{f}L^f=f​
